name: Kim, Sunwook
recent_1: Arippa, F., Barr, A., Phillips, B., Kim, S., Nussbaum, M. A., & Harris-Adamson, C. (2026). Wearing an Arm Support Exoskeleton Does Not Affect Balance but May Decrease Dynamic Stability During a Step-Down Maneuver. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261441959
recent_2: Akinwande, F., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Passive back- and arm-support exoskeletons have effects on physical demands and user perceptions in simulated manual mining tasks that are generally beneficial but are both device- and task-specific. Applied Ergonomics, 137, 104819. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104819
recent_3: Li, L., Behjati Ashtiani, M., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Evaluating model-estimated shoulder muscle activity during overhead work with varied task demands and exoskeleton use. Applied Ergonomics, 137, 104822. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104822
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested whether wearing a passive arm-support exoskeleton compromises postural control during a step-down maneuver, finding no effect on overall balance but a measurable reduction in dynamic stability. The work matters because it probes a potential safety trade-off of occupational exoskeletons—gait and fall risk—beyond their intended benefits for upper-limb work.
recent_2_synopsis: This paper evaluated passive back-support and arm-support exoskeletons across simulated manual mining tasks, quantifying their effects on physical demands (muscle activity, posture) and user perceptions. It shows the benefits are real but highly device- and task-specific, underscoring that exoskeleton selection must be matched to the specific job and not assumed to transfer across tasks.
recent_3_synopsis: Using musculoskeletal modeling, this work estimated shoulder muscle activity during overhead work under varied task demands with and without an arm-support exoskeleton. It advances model-based exposure assessment as a way to predict the biomechanical effects of exoskeletons without requiring extensive in-lab EMG measurement.
seminal_1: Alemi, M. M., Madinei, S., Kim, S., Srinivasan, D., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2020). Effects of Two Passive Back-Support Exoskeletons on Muscle Activity, Energy Expenditure, and Subjective Assessments During Repetitive Lifting. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 62(3), 458-474. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819897669
seminal_2: Madinei, S., Alemi, M. M., Kim, S., Srinivasan, D., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2020). Biomechanical Evaluation of Passive Back-Support Exoskeletons in a Precision Manual Assembly Task: "Expected" Effects on Trunk Muscle Activity, Perceived Exertion, and Task Performance. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 62(3), 441-457. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819890966
seminal_1_synopsis: This study compared two commercial passive back-support exoskeletons during repetitive lifting, measuring trunk muscle activity, metabolic energy expenditure, and subjective ratings to characterize both benefits and unintended costs. With roughly 161 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is among Kim's most influential papers and a widely cited benchmark for evaluating back-support exoskeletons in occupational lifting.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper biomechanically evaluated passive back-support exoskeletons in a precision manual assembly task, testing whether expected reductions in trunk muscle activity hold when the task requires fine manual control, and examining effects on perceived exertion and task performance. With roughly 115 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is a foundational contribution showing that exoskeleton benefits depend on task type and can trade off against precision performance.
expertise_overview: Sunwook Kim studies occupational biomechanics and human-exoskeleton interaction, evaluating how passive and powered back-support and arm-support exoskeletons alter muscle activity, spine and shoulder loads, energy expenditure, and worker-perceived exertion across realistic manual tasks. A central theme is the trade-offs of wearable assistive devices: he examines unintended effects on postural balance, reactive recovery from slips and trips, dynamic stability, and gait alongside the intended physical-demand reductions. He combines laboratory methods such as surface EMG, motion capture, and musculoskeletal modeling with field studies of usability, user acceptance, and adoption barriers in industry. His application domains span automotive assembly and logistics, construction, manual materials handling, and mining. He also works on exposure assessment and postural control, increasingly incorporating model-based and sensor- or vision-driven estimation of physical work demands and musculoskeletal disorder risk.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Sunwook Kim, Research Assistant Professor, Grado Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering, Virginia Tech (occupational biomechanics / Occupational Ergonomics and Biomechanics labs). Confirmed via Virginia Tech ISE faculty page (ise.vt.edu/people/faculty/kim.html), Google Scholar profile (user=hYl39YIAAAAJ; ~4,453 total citations, h-index 35), LinkedIn, and ResearchGate, plus consistency with the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers (recurring co-author Maury A. Nussbaum, index 57; exoskeleton/ergonomics theme). Disambiguated from other 'Sunwook Kim' researchers in robotics/CS by the exoskeleton/occupational-biomechanics topic and Nussbaum co-authorship. All cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching title, Kim in author list): recent_1 (10.1177/00187208261441959, HF 2026), recent_2 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104819, Applied Ergonomics 2026, Akinwande/Kim/Nussbaum), recent_3 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104822, Applied Ergonomics 2026, Li/Behjati Ashtiani/Kim/Nussbaum), seminal_1 (10.1177/0018720819897669, HF 2020, vol 62(3) 458-474), seminal_2 (10.1177/0018720819890966, HF 2020, vol 62(3) 441-457). Seminal citation counts from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17 (seminal_1 ~161; seminal_2 ~115; consistent with the seed hf_papers counts). No unverifiable citations are included in the output.
